title: Snow Flies: The Bug That Makes Its Own Heat in the Cold
summary: Scientists found a special bug called a snow fly that lives in very cold places. Snow flies are insects that walk on snow to find friends and lay eggs. They can stay alive when it is freezing cold, even at -6 degrees Celsius. This is surprising because most insects cannot survive in such cold weather. Snow flies have two cool tricks. First, they make antifreeze proteins like Arctic fish to stop ice from hurting their cells. Second, they can create their own body heat, just like mammals do. This helps them stay active in the snow instead of hiding. The study helps us learn how animals adapt to extreme cold and might help protect things from freezing damage. It was done by scientists at Northwestern University and published in a science journal.

background_read: Snow flies are small insects found in snowy areas. Unlike most insects, they do not have wings and walk on snow. They are active in freezing temperatures, which is unusual because insects are usually cold-blooded and rely on the environment for warmth. This study explores how snow flies use special proteins and heat production to survive, teaching us about adaptation in nature.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists at Northwestern University studied snow flies.
WHAT: Snow flies can survive in freezing cold by making antifreeze proteins and generating their own body heat.
WHERE: The study was conducted at Northwestern University and published in Current Biology journal.
WHY: To understand how life adapts to extreme cold and help protect things from freezing damage.

perspectives.description: The study reveals new biological adaptations in insects for cold survival.
